chunk_summary: Growth plate injury, avascular necrosis, and multifocal disease are critical complications of bone infections, requiring early detection and long-term orthopedic follow-up to prevent permanent disability.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of metaphyseal infections near growth plates on limb development.
Recognize the risks of avascular necrosis in septic arthritis of the hip, especially in young children.
Identify clinical signs and diagnostic strategies for multifocal acute hematogenous osteomyelitis.
Learn the importance of long-term orthopedic surveillance in children with bone infections.
key_facts: Metaphyseal infections near growth plates can cause limb-length discrepancies or angular deformities months after apparent cure.
Septic arthritis of the hip can lead to avascular necrosis due to elevated intracapsular pressure, particularly in infants and young children.
Multifocal acute hematogenous osteomyelitis should be suspected with pain at multiple sites, high inflammatory markers, or slow treatment response.
Whole-body imaging or radionuclide screening may be required to map the full disease burden in multifocal cases.
Follow-up should extend beyond microbiologic cure, with orthopedic surveillance for deformities, gait disturbances, or recurrent pain.
reference_quote: Septic arthritis of the hip is particularly dangerous because elevated intracapsular pressure can compromise blood supply to the femoral head and lead to avascular necrosis. This risk is highest in infants and young children and is a major reason hip drainage must not be delayed.
document_key_topics: Growth plate injury and limb deformities
Orthopedic follow-up in pediatric bone infections
